did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The study used multimodal large language models (ChatGPT-4 and LLaVA-1.6) and deep learning models (VGG16, ResNet50, Swin-B, Model Derm) to perform skin disease identification (classification of melanoma, melanocytic nevi, and benign keratosis-like lesions) from dermatoscopic images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as a 'RESEARCH ARTICLE' and presents original experimental results. The Methods section details the use of specific datasets (HAM10000 subset), models (ChatGPT-4, LLaVA-1.6, VGG16, ResNet50, Swin-B, Model Derm), and statistical analyses (chi-square tests, AOD metrics) to evaluate sex and age biases. The Results section reports specific accuracy and F1-score findings, indicating it is an original study rather than a review.

title: Evaluating Sex and Age Biases in Multimodal Large Language Models for Skin Disease Identification from Dermatoscopic Images
brief_summary: This study evaluated the performance and fairness of two multimodal large language models, ChatGPT-4 and LLaVA-1.6, in identifying three skin diseases (melanoma, melanocytic nevi, and benign keratosis-like lesions) from dermatoscopic images. Using a balanced subset of the HAM10000 dataset, the researchers compared these LLMs against deep learning baselines including VGG16, ResNet50, Model Derm, and Swin-B. The evaluation assessed accuracy and F1-scores across different sex and age groups to determine demographic biases. Results showed that ChatGPT-4 and LLaVA-1.6 outperformed the best CNN-based baseline but were significantly less accurate than the Swin-B vision transformer. Crucially, ChatGPT-4 demonstrated generally unbiased performance across sex and age groups, whereas LLaVA-1.6 exhibited some biases, suggesting LLMs can be useful and fair tools for dermatological screening.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the three skin diseases studied in the HAM10000 subset. The balanced dataset contained 1,112 images of melanoma. It is described as a malignant lesion and considered more serious than MN and BKL.
One of the three skin diseases studied in the HAM10000 subset. The balanced dataset contained 1,115 images of melanocytic nevi.
One of the three skin diseases studied in the HAM10000 subset. The balanced dataset contained 1,100 images of BKL. The paper notes that BKL and melanoma appear quite similar.

all_task_types.target: Skin disease identification (melanoma, melanocytic nevi, or benign keratosis-like lesions)
all_task_types.details: The study performs multi-class classification of dermatoscopic images into three discrete diagnostic categories: melanoma, melanocytic nevi (MN), and benign keratosis-like lesions (BKL). The models (ChatGPT-4, LLaVA-1.6, VGG16, ResNet50, Swin-B, Model Derm) predict discrete disease labels. Performance is evaluated using accuracy and F1-scores for these classes.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A subset of the HAM10000 dataset, an open, multi-source dermatoscopic image dataset. The study focused on three specific skin diseases: melanoma, melanocytic nevi (MN), and benign keratosis-like lesions (BKL). The original dataset contained 10,015 images covering 7 diseases, but this study selected images for the three most common classes to create a balanced subset.

use.training: Used for training VGG16 and ResNet50 models (2,355 images). Also used to generate responses from ChatGPT-4 which were manually interpreted as ground truth for fine-tuning a BERT model.
use.validation: Included within the training set for VGG16 and ResNet50 models, but specific validation split counts were not reported separately.
use.testing: Used for evaluating the performance of ChatGPT-4, LLaVA-1.6, Swin-B, VGG16, ResNet50, Model Derm, and a Random baseline model (972 images).
use.other: Used to assess sex and age biases by categorizing samples into Female/Male/Unknown sex groups and Young/Middle-aged/Senior age groups.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To determine whether an individual's sex or age group is correlated with the accuracy of skin disease identification, by testing the null hypothesis that accuracy measures are the same across groups against the alternative that they differ.

To quantify the distance between the model's performance and the ideal state of Equalized Odds (EOD) by calculating the average prediction bias (difference in True Positive Rates and False Positive Rates) across different demographic groups.
Used as the ideal state criterion for fairness evaluation, requiring equal true positive rates (TPRs) and false positive rates (FPRs) across different groups.
Used as a fairness assessment metric to evaluate bias in the models.
Used as a fairness assessment metric to evaluate bias in the models.
To determine statistical significance for the chi-square tests; if the p-value is less than 0.05, the null hypothesis is rejected.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: The accuracy measures of the demographic groups (sex or age) are the same.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: The accuracy measures differ among the demographic groups.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: If the P value is less than the significance level (0.05), the null hypothesis is rejected, concluding that there is a significant difference in accuracy across the groups.

HAM10000_subset_balanced.description: Fairness evaluation across sex and age groups.
HAM10000_subset_balanced.notes: ChatGPT-4 is generally unbiased in identifying these skin diseases across sex and age groups. LLaVA-1.6 is generally unbiased across age groups but biased across sex groups (specifically for Melanocytic nevi, p=0.005). Swin-B is biased in identifying melanocytic nevi across age groups (p=0.011). VGG16 and ResNet50 showed significant biases across age groups.

architecture_used.main_model: ChatGPT-4, LLaVA-1.6, Swin-B, VGG16, ResNet50, Model Derm
architecture_used.pretraining: LLaVA-1.6 utilizes the LLaMA model from Meta and relies on the pre-trained Contrastive Language-Image Pre-Training (CLIP) visual encoder from OpenAI. Swin-B was pretrained on ImageNet-22K. Model Derm was trained on 220,680 images of 174 disorders. VGG16 and ResNet50 were validated on large-scale datasets.

architecture_details.ChatGPT-4: Proprietary multimodal large language model based on GPT-4.
architecture_details.LLaVA-1.6: Open-sourced multimodal LLM utilizing LLaMA backbone and CLIP visual encoder.
architecture_details.Swin-B: Vision transformer-based architecture using a sliding window mechanism and hierarchical design.
architecture_details.VGG16: Convolutional neural network (CNN) based deep learning model.
architecture_details.ResNet50: Convolutional neural network (CNN) based deep learning model.
architecture_details.Model Derm: Deep learning model specialized in dermatological diagnosis employing CNN architectures.

BERT.purpose: Fine-tuned using manual interpretation of ChatGPT-4 responses as ground truth for automatic response interpretation.

classification_or_output_head.ChatGPT-4: Textual analysis of lesion characteristics, potential diagnoses, and inference about lesion type, manually or automatically interpreted into one of three classes: melanoma, melanocytic nevi, or benign keratosis-like lesions.
classification_or_output_head.LLaVA-1.6: Direct identification of the specific type of lesion.
classification_or_output_head.Swin-B, VGG16, ResNet50, Model Derm: Custom classification layers added for skin disease identification.

preprocessing_techniques: No image preprocessing was applied for data augmentation.
Dataset balancing by selecting samples to make the number of images for the three diseases (melanoma, melanocytic nevi, and benign keratosis-like lesions) roughly equal.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: The original HAM10000 dataset was imbalanced. The researchers created a balanced subset by selecting samples to make the number of images for the three diseases (melanoma, melanocytic nevi, and benign keratosis-like lesions) roughly equal. A threshold of 1,100 samples per disease was set. The final balanced dataset contained 3,327 images: 1,112 melanoma, 1,115 melanocytic nevi, and 1,100 benign keratosis-like lesions.
The paper explicitly states that no image pre-processing was applied for data augmentation.

conclusions: Multimodal large language models (ChatGPT-4 and LLaVA-1.6) demonstrated overall accuracies that were 3% and 23% higher (and F1-scores that were 4% and 34% higher), respectively, than the best-performing CNN-based baseline (VGG16).
ChatGPT-4 and LLaVA-1.6 had accuracies that were 38% and 26% lower (and F1-scores that were 38% and 19% lower), respectively, than the vision transformer model Swin-B.
ChatGPT-4 was generally unbiased in identifying skin diseases across sex and age groups.
LLaVA-1.6 was generally unbiased across age groups but exhibited bias across sex groups.
Swin-B was found to be biased in identifying melanocytic nevi across age groups.
The study suggests the usefulness and fairness of LLMs in dermatological applications for aiding physicians and practitioners with diagnostic recommendations and patient screening.
Further experiments using larger and more diverse datasets are needed to verify and evaluate the reliability and fairness of LLMs in healthcare.
important_remarks: The study is limited by the use of a single dataset (HAM10000 subset) with ambiguous patient contribution records, which may lead to overestimating model performance and failing to represent real-world clinical heterogeneity.

Experiments were conducted only once, meaning observed biases may be attributed to underlying uncertainty rather than systematic model flaws; repeated experiments and sensitivity analysis are recommended.

The dataset was artificially balanced to mitigate sample size disparities, which may not reflect real-world clinical distributions where melanoma prevalence is typically higher than benign keratosis-like lesions.
ChatGPT-4 responses required manual interpretation to extract diagnoses due to safety filters avoiding direct medical diagnoses, introducing potential subjectivity in ground truth labeling.
The study did not evaluate race or ethnicity biases, as the dataset lacked this demographic information, limiting the generalizability of fairness conclusions.

LLaVA-1.6 exhibited significant bias across sex groups for melanocytic nevi identification (p=0.005), potentially due to imbalanced selective training data, particularly lower accuracy for younger and female groups.
Swin-B showed bias in identifying melanocytic nevi across age groups (p=0.011), indicating that even high-performing vision transformers are not immune to demographic biases.
VGG16 and ResNet50 demonstrated significant biases across age groups, highlighting persistent fairness issues in traditional CNN-based baselines.
The study acknowledges that using synthetic data generated by tools like DALL-E-3 could address privacy and feasibility issues but was not implemented in this work.

Performance comparisons with other studies (e.g., Zhou et al., Kaczmarczyk et al.) are complicated by differences in input types (real-life photos vs. dermatoscopic images), task complexity, and lack of fairness evaluation in prior works.
